insights: Ed Gompf manages 76 IT employees (74% of the department), while Mariano Maury manages only 25 (24%), creating a 3:1 managerial imbalance that directly exposes Ed to unsustainable user management overload.

Data Analysts—the largest IT role group—are overwhelmingly concentrated under Ed Gompf (27 of 32, or 87%), making role-type skew the primary driver of the imbalance rather than broad departmental growth or random assignment.

Ed Gompf holds exclusive managerial responsibility across 17 locations—including all Asia-Pacific (Tokyo, Shanghai, Melbourne) and Latin American sites (Cancun, Medellin, Brasilia)—where Mariano has zero assigned staff, indicating structural geographic silos that compound Ed’s span-of-control burden.

Ed’s geographic footprint spans 23 locations with an average of 3.17 reports per location, nearly double Mariano’s 1.92, reflecting significantly higher coordination overhead from fragmented, low-density assignments rather than centralized team clustering.
The imbalance is not attributable to data quality issues: only 2 IT records have missing managers (the managers themselves), confirming the 76:25 ratio is complete, accurate, and operationally real.

Although Ed’s 27 Data Analysts are distributed across 12 locations—not clustered in one region—their role-based concentration remains fully decoupled from geography, revealing that functional role allocation (not location logistics) is the dominant governance failure.
summary: The IT department exhibits a severe and operationally consequential managerial imbalance: Ed Gompf oversees 76 employees (74%) while Mariano Maury manages just 25 (24%), creating acute risk of user management overload for Ed. This 3:1 disparity is driven primarily by two structural factors—87% of all Data Analysts report to Ed, and he exclusively manages staff across 17 locations where Mariano has no presence, including all Asia-Pacific and Latin American sites. Ed’s broader geographic footprint (23 locations vs. Mariano’s 12) and higher average span per location (3.17 vs. 1.92) further amplify coordination strain. Critically, this imbalance is not an artifact of missing data—only two IT records lack manager assignments, and both are the managers themselves—confirming the distribution reflects actual governance design. To correct this, immediate focus should be placed on rebalancing Data Analyst assignments and expanding Mariano’s geographic coverage into high-concentration regions currently managed solely by Ed.
